subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The chapter is organised entirely around fractions as equal parts of a unit whole and as divisions of whole numbers. The dhokla story progressively builds unit fractions (1/2, 1/3, 1/4, 1/5) by sharing one circular whole among increasing numbers of guests; paper-folding with rectangular sheets establishes that one half and two quarters are equivalent; the fraction chart and fraction kit are used to compare fractions such as 1/6 and 1/8 and to verify that smaller denominators mean larger shares. The garden and dosa activities extend this to non-unit fractions (2/5, 3/5, 3/8) and to fractions of discrete collections such as 1/4 of 8 diyas and 1/3 of 9 friends, all of which map directly to the competency of representing and comparing commonly used fractions in daily life.

Arithmetic operations underpin several tasks in the chapter: finding what fraction each child receives when 12 cookies are shared among 3, 6, 2, or 4 children requires division of whole numbers; finding 1/4 of 16 barfis requires division and understanding its relationship to equal grouping. These tasks position fraction as the result of dividing one whole number by another, substantively engaging the competency of understanding arithmetic operations and applying them to solve daily-life problems — a strand distinct from the part-whole representation work.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Several tasks in the chapter are framed as real-life word problems requiring problem-solving rather than pure fraction representation: the dhokla scenario asks students to reason about Sumedha's combined share when two guests give their portions back; the ribbon problem requires deducing Rohan's ribbon length given that Mohan's is one-fourth of it; the birthday hairband and pot-of-flowers tasks ask students to apply fractional reasoning to discrete everyday objects. These engage the competency of solving daily-life problems using mathematical operations.

Comparing unit fractions without computation — deciding which is larger among 1/5 and 1/4, or whether 2/3 is greater than 1/2 using the fraction chart — requires selecting and applying reasoning strategies rather than paper-pencil calculation. The fraction-kit and fraction-chart discussion tasks explicitly ask students to estimate and verify size relationships between fractions using visual tools, engaging the competency of selecting appropriate methods for computing, including estimation.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to represent and compare fractions as equal parts of a unit whole or a discrete collection by moving from concrete paper-folding and dhokla-sharing experiences through pictorial models (fraction chart, garden strips, dosa circles) to symbolic notation of unit and non-unit fractions.
